data.title: U.S. stocks fall as tech sector struggles, rate-cut uncertainties rattle markets
data.description:
Wall Street's major indexes ended lower on Wednesday, marking a second day of declines, as struggling U.S. technology stocks weighed on the markets and uncertainty persisted among investors.

The S&P 500 (SP500) closed -0.3%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) ended -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) finished -0.4%.

This “disappointing day” for the markets “was in spite of some positive news in the housing market,” Daniel Jones, investing group leader of Crude Value Insights, told Seeking Alpha.

“My suspicion is that the market is digesting [Federal Reserve rate cuts] news and recognizing that, while rate cuts are positive in the long run, the economic situation that we find ourselves in is precarious,” Jones added.

“With jobs data continuing to come in weak, consumer confidence near multi-year lows, and other data looking disappointing even as inflation looks to be on the rise because of tariffs, I suspect more weakness will occur at some point. And that's simply because I anticipate the U.S. economy entering into a recession in the coming months,” he said.

On the tech front, OpenAI is considering renting AI chips from Nvidia (NVDA) -0.8% to reduce costs associated with the new $100B data center partnership between the two companies.

Micron Technology (MU) edged lower -2.8%, even after its outlook eclipsed estimates on AI data center demand. Adobe (ADBE) -2.4% and Oracle (ORCL) -1.6% are also pushing the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) lower.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.15%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.61%. A $70B, 5-Year Note Auction slightly tailed.

On the economy, new home sales surged 20.5% MoM in August to 800k. Consensus was for 649k.

Business uncertainty about revenue growth rose to 3.83% in September, up from 3.77% in August, according to an Atlanta Fed survey. Also, CFOs recorded greater optimism in Q3, the Richmond Fed survey showed.

Traders will now await jobless claims data on Thursday and PCE inflation data on Friday.

U.S. stocks ended in the red on Tuesday, breaking from their three-day all-time high closes, after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell largely reiterated his message from last week that policymakers will have a difficult path ahead on interest rates.

data.title: TNF Pharmaceuticals announces name change to Q/C Technologies
data.description:
• TNF Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:TNFA) announced that it has changed its corporate name to Q/C Technologies.
  • The stock will begin trading under the new ticker symbol “QCLS” on Nasdaq, effective as of the market open on Thursday, September 25, 2025
  • The new name Q/C Technologies represents the company's recent strategic pivot into quantum-class computing, it said.

data.title: Costco FQ4 earnings: What to expect
data.description:
Costco’s (NASDAQ:COST) fourth-quarter revenue is expected to jump 8% as the company prepares to report earnings on September 24, after market close.

The company is projected to report a revenue of $86.06 billion, while the consensus EPS estimate is $5.80.  

Seeking Alpha analyst Luca Socci noted that the company’s solid comparable sales and membership growth remain in focus ahead of the earnings.

Costco reported that yearly comparable sales were up 5.9% YoY, with North America up 6.2%, showing a counter trend among peers who reported stronger international growth but saw weakness in North America.

Socci sees Costco’s annual revenue coming up to just above $275B.

“I expect Costco's net income margin to be between 2.9% and 3%, which means its net income will be close to, if not slightly above, $8B. Since Costco doesn't do buybacks, the share count is flat at 443M. Therefore, the projected EPS is $18.01. The result is a PE of 52,” he added.

Despite the encouraging financial metrics, the analyst rated COST as a Hold over valuation concerns. The stock has lost nearly 21% on a YTD basis, while in the past year it has declined a little over 10%.

Over the last 1 year, COST has beaten EPS estimates 75% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 25% of the time. Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 4 upward revisions and 11 downward moves. Revenue estimates have seen 9 upward revisions and 5 downward moves.

data.title: Trump to sign TikTok deal on Thursday — White House
data.description:
• White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said Wednesday that President Donald Trump will sign the TikTok (BDNCE) deal on Thursday.
  • After agreeing on a deal framework for the popular short video content platform with Chinese officials, the White House ensured that American buyers have full control over the app's recommendation software once its parent, ByteDance, divests the U.S. operations of TikTok. 
  • As part of the new deal, U.S. owners of TikTok would license the algorithm from ByteDance, and software giant Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) would retrain it "from the ground up."

data.title: U.S. lawmakers raise concerns on ESPN's recent NFL and MLB deals
data.description:
Democratic senators, led by Elizabeth Warren, have sent a letter to top executives of the media and entertainment giant Disney (NYSE:DIS), raising concerns that the recent deals by ESPN with the NFL and the NHL could restrict competition and raise prices for sports fans. 

ESPN in August reached a deal with the NFL for its media assets, including NFL Network, the NFL's linear RedZone Channel, and NFL Fantasy—in exchange for a 10% stake in the Disney-owned sports network. It also struck a $1.65B three-year deal with Major League Baseball, under which it would incorporate MLB.TV into its new direct-to-consumer streaming service.

Warren, along with senators Bernie Sanders, Joaquin Castro, and Patrick Ryan, said in the letter that "ESPN’s deals with the NFL and the MLB would give the sports media giant new degrees of control…for two of the most profitable sports leagues in the country. We have serious concerns that fans and competitors will suffer as a result."

The lawmakers have asked the executives to get back with their queries by October 8, 2025.

data.title: Senate Finance Committee to hold crypto taxation hearing next month
data.description:
The U.S. Senate Committee on Finance will be holding a hearing to assess the taxation of digital assets on Oct. 1, Chairman Mike Crapo said in a statement Wednesday.

The hearing comes as the Trump administration moves ahead with its efforts to make the U.S. a more pro-crypto regulatory environment, with stricter, Biden-era rules getting scrapped and replaced with friendlier ones. 

Earlier in the month, the New York State Department of Financial Services recently issued new guidance on the use of blockchain analytics tools for banks weighing or already pursuing crypto-linked activities.

Also in September, the U.S. and U.K. were reportedly preparing a deal to coordinate more closely on crypto and stablecoins after talks between Chancellor Rachel Reeves and U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

data.title: Xcel Energy surges after reaching $640M settlement in 2021 Colorado wildfire
data.description:
Xcel Energy (NASDAQ:XEL) +5.8% in Wednesday's trading, hitting an all-time intraday high of $77.47, after saying it reached settlement agreements that resolve all litigation related to the 2021 Marshall Fire in Colorado, ahead of a trial that was scheduled to begin this week in a Boulder County court.

Xcel (NASDAQ:XEL) said it expects to pay $640 million related to the settlements, with $350 million funded by its remaining insurance coverage and none from its customers.

The company said it does not admit any fault, wrongdoing or negligence in connection with the resolution, maintaining its position that its equipment did not cause or contribute to the fire.

Xcel (XEL) has said it expects to recognize a $290 million charge to its Q3 earnings related to the Marshall Fire, which caused an estimated $2 billion in property losses.

data.title: Pizza Pizza Royalty declares CAD 0.0775 dividend
data.description:

data.title: Medline evaluating $5B IPO filing as soon as next month - report
data.description:
Medical supplies marker Medline is evaluating filing publicly for an initial public offering as soon as next month.

Medline may raise about $5 billion in an IPO and could be valued at about $50 billion, according to a  Bloomberg report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.The medical supplies firm has private equity firms Blackstone (BX), Carlyle Group (CG) and Hellman & Friedman as its main backers. 

An October filing would allow the company to go public as soon as November, according to the report. Goldman Sachs (GS) and Morgan Stanley (MS) are the leading banks working on the IPO. Medline in December filed confidentially for a listing. 

Medline didn't immediately respond to Bloomberg request for comment.

Medline, which sells such medical staples as surgical gloves, gowns, masks and lab supplies, was sold by the Mills family in 2021 to Blackstone (BX), The Carlyle Group (CG) and Hellman & Friedman for $34 billion.

data.title: Chicago Fed's Goolsbee cautions against front-loading rate cuts - report
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago President Austan Goolsbee warned that the U.S. central bank should not rush into multiple interest-rate cuts, citing lingering inflation concerns despite a cooling jobs market, according to a Wednesday media report.

Goolsbee supported the policy-setting Federal Open Market Committee's decision last week to cut rates for the first time this year, he said in an interview with the Financial Times. He backed the FOMC move due to signs that U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs were not proving as inflationary as feared.

Going forward, though, “I’m uncomfortable with overly frontloading a lot of rate cuts on the presumption that [inflation] will probably just be transitory and go away,” Goolsbee said, pointing to concerns across Midwest firms that inflation still was not tamed.

The inflationary impact of tariffs would likely be short-lived, the Chicago Fed chief told the FT, though inflation has surpassed the Fed's 2% goal for 4.5 years and was "now heading the wrong way."
